verte.title: Verte
verte.intro: The reference frame: alpine, bitter, bright, and louche-friendly. This concept preserves the classic trinity of wormwood, anise, and fennel, then uses late green botanicals to widen the aromatic halo.
verte.sensoryBody: Start with high herbal lift, move into cool anise sweetness, and finish with dry wormwood bitterness. The palate should read green rather than sugary, with a louche that looks opaline jade.
herbs: sweet aromatic spine and louche support
resinous green top notes and classic color contribution
fresh citron lift without breaking style
molecules: the core anise-fennel sweetness and louche behavior
dry, slightly camphoraceous contrast to anethole
floral lift that softens the bitter frame
lemon balm brightness in the top note
green visual identity in naturally colored variants
verte.colorBody: The visual identity comes from post-base green botanicals rather than artificial coloring: hyssop, melissa, and pontica all reinforce the classic verte signal.

rouge.title: Rouge
rouge.intro: A red-leaning absinthe concept that keeps wormwood and anise intact while adding hibiscus for tart floral color and a brighter, more wine-like arc.
rouge.sensoryBody: The opening should suggest cranberry, pomegranate, and rose without turning into a liqueur. Bitterness stays present, but the mid-palate picks up hibiscus snap and a vivid ruby impression.
herbs: optional support for a fuller floral bridge
keeps the top note lively and not jammy
molecules: red hue from hibiscus and pH-sensitive vibrancy
rosy lift when petals or rosehip are present
floral smoothing across the transition from anise to hibiscus
rouge.colorBody: Hibiscus does the heavy lifting, delivering a saturated crimson tone. The red identity works best when the visual aim is jewel-like rather than opaque.

bleue.title: Bleue
bleue.intro: A clear absinthe concept that skips the green coloring step and instead leans into precision, brightness, and a clean louche on dilution.
bleue.sensoryBody: Crystal-clear in the bottle and silvery-opaline when diluted. The aroma should be focused, brisk, and elegant, with fewer leafy tones than a verte and a slightly more architectural bitterness.
herbs: optional nuance without visible green extraction
molecules: essential for louche and sweet spice shape
crisp terpene lift from supporting botanicals
subtle aromatic bridge through the mid-palate
bleue.colorBody: The identity is defined by restraint: no green coloration phase, no red infusion, just a transparent spirit that blooms when water reveals the louche.

detour.title: Detour
detour.intro: A deliberate departure from the usual anise-led licorice profile. Lemon balm carries the freshness, butterfly pea handles the chromatic drama, and the overall shape reads herbal-citrus rather than confectionary.
detour.sensoryBody: More like a mountain cordial crossed with an absinthe idea: cool herbs, citrus leaf, soft bitterness, and a color shift from blue toward purple when acidity enters the glass.
herbs: primary aromatic signature and green-citrus lift
blue chromatic source and theatrical shift
keeps an absinthe echo without heavy licorice
molecules: lemon-balm brightness and the main top-note cue
herbal depth often associated with lemon balm
butterfly pea pigments responsible for blue-to-purple shift
supportive herbal complexity from verbena-style botanicals
enough spine to keep it from reading like tea
detour.colorTitle: Why it shifts blue to purple
detour.colorBody: Butterfly pea contributes blue anthocyanin-like pigments that visually swing purple in a more acidic environment. Lemon elements support the narrative even when the acid source sits outside the core herb bill.
